Replace.Text: For this Age, gain +15% [icon:YIELD_CULTURE] Culture at the cost of -10% [icon:YIELD_Science] Science
For this Age, +3 Combat Strength for your Naval Units at the cost of -10% [icon:YIELD_DIPLOMACY] Influence
For this Age, gain +15% [icon:YIELD_CULTURE] Culture at the cost of -10% [icon:YIELD_Science] Science
For this Age, +3 Combat Strength for your Air Units at the cost of -10% Influence[icon:YIELD_DIPLOMACY]
Legend Unlock. +15% [icon:YIELD_PRODUCTION] Production towards constructing [icon:CITY_BUILDING_LIST] Buildings and [icon:WONDER] Wonders with [icon:NAR_REW_GREATWORK] Great Works slots.
Legend Unlock. +5 [icon:NAR_REW_COMBAT] Combat Strength for all Units against [icon:CITY_FORTIFIED] Fortified Districts.

Alexander III, later known as Alexander the Great, was born in 356 BCE to Philip II of Macedon and his fourth wife Olympias. Eager and ambitious even as a youth, Alexander’s teenage years hinted at his outsized ambitions. At 13, he wagered his father that he could tame a well-bred but unruly horse. Seeing the horse was afraid of his own shadow, Alexander turned him toward the sun, spoke to him calmly, and successfully mounted and rode the horse. Bucephalus, as he named him, would go on to be one of Alexander’s closest companions throughout his campaign, even warranting a lavish funeral after the horse succumbed to battle wounds. Alexander’s first independent campaign came when he was just 15 years old – he successfully put down a rebellion and founded his first city Alexandropolis.

When Philip was murdered at a wedding banquet in 336 BCE, Alexander took over the mantle of general in the Greeks’ war against Persia. Nominally, the war’s aim was to enact vengeance on the Persians for sacking Athens over 150 years previously, but to Alexander, it was the first step in conquering Asia. When he crossed over the Hellespont, he is said to have struck his sword into the soil and announced his gratitude to the gods for gifting him Asia. Later, when moving further inland, he stopped at Gordion where a well-known tale told of an old ox-cart tied by a complex knot. It was said that whoever could untie the knot would go on to rule all of Asia. Plutarch tells us there were conflicting reports of how Alexander fulfilled the prophecy: most claimed that he sliced the knot through with his sword, "solving" the puzzle and claiming his prize. Whatever the truth, Alexander made his case for King of Asia.

Darius III, Persia's Great King, sent his generals to intercept the Macedonian force, but they were defeated at the Granicus River, in the first example of what would become Alexander's battle strategy. He used both infantry and cavalry, deftly taking advantage of their unique strengths to rely more aggressively on cavalry than was typical. With his Companions, the elite cavalry force of the Macedonians, he would lead the charge on the right flank, driving the enemy army into confusion. Macedonian phalanxes and Greek hoplites formed the infantry portion of the army. Equipped with the sarissa, a long pike, they would close in on the enemy army following the charge, entrapping enemy forces. Later, in the Battle of Issus, Darius tried new tactics, but Alexander won again. Two years later in their final encounter at Gaugamela, again Alexander won and though Darius escaped, he was assassinated not much later. Where Darius went wrong was partially in his military strategy and lack of experience but also in his inability to recognize that Alexander had an insatiable thirst for conquest. Darius offered peace three times, and each time, Alexander refused.

Our ancient sources for Alexander’s life are predominantly Greek and critical of Alexander’s adoption of non-Greek cultural practices. In Persia, Alexander began wearing some (but not all) of the royal attire, including the diadem which would become symbolic of Hellenistic rulers. Persians practiced proskynesis, bowing low to the ground in front of their ruler. Highly inappropriate for Greeks who valued equality among men and would only lower themselves before a god, Alexander allowed and even encouraged this behavior among the Persians. Greeks claimed he had far too much hubris and thought himself truly divine. Alexander did claim ancestry from Achilles and Hercules (Heracles), and thus from Zeus, and in Egypt, he claimed the Egyptian god Ammon (Amun) as his father when he visited his shrine just as Hercules was said to have done. Though sacrilegious for democratic Greeks, divine kingship was nothing unusual for a Macedonian. It was accepted (and even expected) that the ruler would have a divine tie.

The Greek perspective on Alexander would color historians' outlooks on him throughout the modern period, leading to a host of monographs on Alexander’s decadence and divine delusions. Today, our outlook has shifted to be more balanced and practical, seeing as it is more likely that Alexander strategically adjusted his practice to suit the many peoples of his realm and evidence suggests he was well aware of his mortal status. Throughout his life, Alexander continued to behave as any pious Macedonian would, even getting up from his deathbed to offer morning sacrifices and pay the respect owed to the gods, an act inconsistent with a man believing himself divine. Our modern understanding of him is one of an arrogant but not delusional man – a leader who deftly managed the contradictory demands of his Macedonian and Persian forces and one who understood his divine birthright in the context of mortality.

Alexander’s campaign started with Asia, but at its height, it reached across the Aegean, the Levant, and Egypt, and stretched eastward across modern Iran and Afghanistan to the Hypasis River in northern India. Though Alexander wanted to continue to the Ganges and beyond, his army refused to fight any longer. Turning back, he marched his men along the coast of the Indian Ocean and back to Persia. His desire for conquest encompassed the world, and had fate seen fit to leave him alive, perhaps he would have achieved that goal. Alas, his mortality was proven when he came down with an illness after a drinking party, likely a local strain of malaria or typhus. Convinced he would recover, he made no plans for succession and instead spent time charting a future campaign across North Africa that he never completed. He died in 323 BCE at 32 years old, leaving an expansive empire in his wake to be fought over for decades by his subordinates.

Alexander had managed to spread Greek culture from the Aegean to India in less than 15 years. He and his men had covered an estimated 22,000 miles through some of the most challenging terrain on the planet. His empire laid the foundation for the eastern Roman Empire (Rome would conquer it a couple hundred years later), and the cities he founded would go on to be instrumental in the spread of science and philosophy. It is no exaggeration to say that Alexander is one of the key reasons Greece has had such a fundamental impact on Western civilization.
One day, the Muses will sing of me – godlike Alexander, the greatest of the Greek heroes.
Greetings, my soon-to-be subject. You can bow or not, whichever is your preference.
Greetings, my soon-to-be subject. You can bow or not, whichever is your preference.
Greetings, my soon-to-be subject. You can bow or not, whichever is your preference.
I am conquering the world. You are part of the world.
Excellent! Enemies who strike first fall all the faster.
I had such plans! Now the world will have no hero.
A fair offer. I will accept your autonomy in this case.
Where did you come up with this? On the krepis?
After the victory festival, we march. The rest of the world awaits.

[icon:YIELD_CITIES] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_CITIES_TOOLTIP]Cities[/TIP] other than your Capital with a [icon:WONDER] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_WONDERS_TOOLTIP]Wonder[/TIP] get +10% [icon:YIELD_PRODUCTION] Production and [icon:YIELD_CULTURE] Culture, doubled if they are on a different Continent than your [icon:NOTIFICATION_SELECT_CAPITAL] [icon:NOTIFICATION_SELECT_CAPITAL] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_CAPITAL_TOOLTIP]Capital[/TIP]. +2 [icon:NAR_REW_COMBAT] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_COMBAT_STRENGTH_TOOLTIP]Combat Strength[/TIP] for [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_MILITARY_UNIT_TOOLTIP]Land Military Units[/TIP]. +1 [icon:NAR_REW_COMBAT] Combat Strength for Unique Military Units for every [icon:YIELD_CITIES] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_CITIES_TOOLTIP]City[/TIP] with a [icon:WONDER] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_WONDERS_TOOLTIP]Wonder[/TIP] other than your Capital (max 6). +2 [icon:MILITARY_VP] Dominion for every [icon:YIELD_CITIES] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_CITIES_TOOLTIP]City[/TIP] with a [icon:WONDER] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_WONDERS_TOOLTIP]Wonder[/TIP] other than your Capital. [icon:YIELD_TOWNS] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_TOWN_TOOLTIP]Towns[/TIP] converted into [icon:YIELD_CITIES] Cities are renamed after Alexander.

[BLIST][LI][icon:YIELD_CITIES] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_CITIES_TOOLTIP]Cities[/TIP] other than your Capital with a [icon:WONDER] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_WONDERS_TOOLTIP]Wonder[/TIP] get [B]+10% [icon:YIELD_PRODUCTION] Production[/B] and [icon:YIELD_CULTURE] [B]Culture[/B], doubled if they are on a different Continent than your [icon:NOTIFICATION_SELECT_CAPITAL] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_CAPITAL_TOOLTIP]Capital[/TIP]. [LI][B]+2[/B] [icon:NAR_REW_COMBAT] [B][TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_COMBAT_STRENGTH_TOOLTIP]Combat Strength[/TIP][/B] for [B]Land [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_MILITARY_UNIT_TOOLTIP]Military Units[/TIP][/B]. [B]+1 [icon:NAR_REW_COMBAT] Combat Strength[/B] for [B]Unique Military Units[/B] for every [icon:YIELD_CITIES] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_CITIES_TOOLTIP]City[/TIP] with a [icon:WONDER] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_WONDERS_TOOLTIP]Wonder[/TIP] other than your Capital (max 6). [LI][B]+2 [icon:MILITARY_VP] Dominion[/B] for every [icon:YIELD_CITIES] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_CITIES_TOOLTIP]City[/TIP] with a [icon:WONDER] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_WONDERS_TOOLTIP]Wonder[/TIP] other than your Capital. [LI][icon:YIELD_TOWNS] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_TOWN_TOOLTIP]Towns[/TIP] converted into [icon:YIELD_CITIES] [B]Cities[/B] are renamed after Alexander.[/LIST]
Likes Leaders with the fewest conquered Settlements. Dislikes Leaders with the most conquered Settlements.

Sing, Muse, of the King of the World, the Hero of Hellas, Son of Zeus and Ammon. From the hills of Macedonia to the edges of Oceanus, your name will mark the bounds of civilization. Take the reins, Alexander. Lead the charge and fulfill your fate.

[B]+1 [icon:YIELD_CULTURE][/B] and [icon:YIELD_PRODUCTION] per [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_AGE_TOOLTIP]Age[/TIP] on [icon:WONDER] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_WONDERS_TOOLTIP]Wonders[/TIP] in [icon:YIELD_CITIES] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_CITIES_TOOLTIP]Cities[/TIP] other than your [icon:NOTIFICATION_SELECT_CAPITAL] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_CAPITAL_TOOLTIP]Capital[/TIP].

[B]+1 [icon:SETTLEMENT_LIMIT][/B] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_SETTLEMENT_LIMIT_TOOLTIP]Settlement Limit[/TIP] in each [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_AGE_TOOLTIP]Age[/TIP]. [B]+1 [icon:YIELD_GOLD][/B] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_MAINTENANCE_TOOLTIP]Maintenance[/TIP] per Age on all [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_LAND_UNITS_TOOLTIP]Land Units[/TIP].

[B]+3 [icon:NAR_REW_COMBAT][/B] [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_COMBAT_STRENGTH_TOOLTIP]Combat Strength[/TIP] for [TIP:LOC_PEDIA_CONCEPTS_CAVALRY_UNITS_TOOLTIP]Cavalry Units[/TIP], but [B]+25% [icon:YIELD_PRODUCTION][/B] and Purchase cost for Cavalry Units.
Unlock at Alexander The Great level 2
Unlock at Alexander The Great level 5
Unlock at Alexander The Great level 9
As Alexander, complete 4 Major Triumphs in a single Antiquity Age.
As Alexander, complete 4 Major Triumphs in a single Exploration Age.
As Alexander, complete the Wonders of the Ancient World Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Orphic Mysteries Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Pax Imperatoria Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the What a Gaius Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Silk Roads Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Greed is Golden Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Library of Ashurbanipal Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Round Earther Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Pulling the Strings Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Bread and Circuses Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Four Corners of the World Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Tower of Babel Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Toshakhana Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Kingdom of Heaven Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Non Sufficit Orbis Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Pax Mongolica Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Treasure Fleet Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Let's Calicut A Deal Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Enlightenment Triumph.
On the Revolutions of the Celestial Spheres
As Alexander, complete the On the Revolutions of the Celestial Spheres Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Order of Assassins Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Quadruple Alliance Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Hic Sunt Dracones Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the We have Habsburgs at Home Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Geographic Society Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Wonderland Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Moral Imperative Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Enemy of the World Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Railroad Tycoon Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Workshop of the World Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Dawn of the Space Race Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Beyond the Sword Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the League of Nations Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Enigma Code Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Sun Never Sets Triumph.
As Alexander, complete the Megalopolis Triumph.
As Alexander, finish the Antiquity Age.
As Alexander, finish the Antiquity Age twice.
As Alexander, finish the Antiquity Age thrice.
As Alexander, finish the Exploration Age.
As Alexander, finish the Exploration Age twice.
As Alexander, finish the Exploration Age thrice.
As Alexander, finish the Modern Age.
As Alexander, finish the Modern Age twice.
As Alexander, finish the Modern Age thrice.
As Alexander, complete 5 Major Triumphs across any number of Ages.
As Alexander, complete 25 Major Triumphs across any number of Ages.
As Alexander in Antiquity, complete a quest to conquer for the gods.
As Alexander in Antiquity, complete a quest to honor the Muses.
As Alexander in Exploration, complete a quest to fulfill fate.
As Alexander in Exploration, complete a quest to inspire awe and loyalty.
As Alexander in Modern, complete a quest to claim the horizon.
As Alexander in Modern, complete a quest to bring legends to life.
As Alexander, achieve a Cultural victory.
As Alexander, achieve a Militaristic victory.
As Alexander, achieve an Economic victory.
As Alexander, achieve a Scientific victory.
As Alexander, achieve a victory on Deity level of Difficulty.

The horseman Genghis Khan strikes Alexander as an odious and arrogant man. His cavalry maraud across the landscape as if it belongs to them. Admittedly, their horses are purebred and quick - a shame the riders are barbarians.
Highest Level Commander Receives the Shock Tactics promotion (or receives enough Experience to gain a level)
This world has room for only one conqueror.
Conquer a Settlement from Genghis Khan.

Wine flows at a banquet as {1_CIVILIZATION_ADJ} thinkers discuss recent advancements in writing. One scholar suggests it could be a form for recording stories of {2_CIVILIZATION_ADJ} heroes or even of Alexander. A blind bard sings from the corner, a smile turning his lips.
Bring a writer on future campaigns.
Writing is a tool for merchants.
Commission a copy of the bard's tale.

It has been a grueling campaign, and Alexander’s army seems ready to mutiny at any moment. Over the course of the war, many local women have joined the camp, yet battle has left no room for celebration. Perhaps a night of levity will cure their war-weary spirits.
Marry everyone who wishes. Open the casks!
They have waited this long. Continue the march.
{1_reward} on Cavalry Units, but 33 Damage to all Cavalry Units.

Swift steeds and shining bronze mark the Hetairoi as they charge across the battlefield, wreaking havoc on enemy lines. {1_CIVILIZATION_ADJ} horses are of exceptional stock, worthy of the elite warriors who ride them. No other general can claim such a loyal force.
A man’s horse is his closest companion.
Only ride with men you trust.

Rumors swirl that Alexander cannot be harmed. Battle after battle, he avoids lethal blows and quickly recovers from any injury. The eyes of the Gorgon on his shield glisten as if alive. The men eagerly await the next fight, though some eye the Gorgon from a distance.
Such a divine gift marks a true hero.
Offer sacrifices for more good fortune.

The last of the old Greek settlements has fallen. Her philosophers and her countrymen now answer to Alexander; her people are lost in the sea of {1_CIVILIZATION_ADJ} faces. All things end in time, yet the world seems to sigh at what might have been.
Hellas will be the new heart of the realm.
{1_STORY_CITY} becomes your Capital and Unrest is removed. {2_Capital} becomes a City if allowed.
Marble can be repurposed for {1_CIVILIZATION_ADJ} needs.
Integrate the Greeks into the army.

The long sarissas of Alexander’s phalanxes may look unwieldy to an outsider, but even the famed Greek hoplite falls before its reach. Their long shields cannot withstand the pressure of rows of pikes, and even Greeks feel fear.
Little will be lost when the Greeks fall.
Their poetry is worth saving at least.

The King and his teacher walk the paths of the Acropolis, discussing a point of contention. Aristotle claims that men should rule and be ruled alike, though non-Greeks are best ruled by a superior Greek king. Alexander considers the best course for one who would rule all.
There are Greeks and there are barbarians.
All peoples live and die alike.

So this Xerxes then is the lavish and decadent ruler who claims kingship over a host of barbarians. He seems concerned more with his attire than his defense, with his crown than his kingdom. Alexander had hoped to find a worthy foe.
Fine garments are for those who lack wisdom.

Alexander listens as a bard recites the story of the great Achilles so beloved of the gods. His might, his rage - that most-feared warrior who stalked the fields of Troy. Yet too, he did sing and grieve for Patroclus, and the sweet fate of long life tempted even him.
He was a hero because of his valor.
The gods love those who bring them gifts.
The gods' blessings are known to all.
Heroes of the past are tales for children.

Across the battlefield, Alexander charges, first in line to strike terror in the enemy's hearts. One by one they fall, the few survivors carrying word of the great king and his overwhelming might. A tremor of fear ripples across the landscape.
Claim their land. The gods will it.
The taste of victory awakens a thirst for conquest.
Capture a Settlement you did not Found.
Coin can calm a raging heart. Temporarily.

Musicians, actors, and bards are gathered in {1_capital}, ready to compete for the prize awarded to the most talented. The city is full of eager spectators, enchanted by the many tales and songs that feature Alexander himself.
Create a permanent position dedicated to the Muses.
Take a bow with the actors.
The ground hums with the march of Alexander’s soldiers as they cascade through the fallen capital. Xerxes’ grand palace stands at the city center, waiting to accept its fate.
Loot the treasure and leave it to rot.
Burn away the stench of Xerxes.
{1_reward}, but a random Constructible in {2_STORY_CITY} is Pillaged.
A Great Work slot is added to {1_STORY_CITY}.

Long has Bucephalus, Alexander’s trusted horse, accompanied him in battle. Together, they have fought and conquered. But now, he lies prone, mortally wounded, and the physician shakes his head mournfully. How can a man withstand such grief!
Host a grand funeral for the greatest of companions.
Celebrate his well-lived life with a feast.
Within the borders of Alexander’s new city, an old oxcart is tied with an impossible knot. Legend says that whoever can loosen the knot and untie the cart will rule the continent.
Slice the knot in half. That will loosen it.
Remove the cart’s yoke to reveal the rope’s ends.

A group of gymnosophists live in this region, and Alexander is eager to hear how their notions differ from those of {1_CIVILIZATION_ADJ} philosophers. One of the naked ascetics proposes a riddle contest. It seems matching wits is popular everywhere.
What came first - night or day?
Which is stronger - life or death?
Conquest leaves no time for riddles.

“Day came first, of course, by exactly one night,” answers the first gymnosophist. “Nonsense,” claims another, “night came first, by exactly one day.” Alexander shakes his head and asks another question.
How can a man become a god?
How long is a life well-lived?

The ascetic speaks quietly. “The dead are countless, yet invisible. Life is stronger as it holds all their miseries.” Another ponders, “Life is like the sun - strong at its height, then weaker as it falls and ultimately fades away.” Satisfied, Alexander asks another.
What is the wisest of animals?
What is the cruelest of beasts?

A lively debate has followed Alexander’s question, for none can truly know the measure of man or beast. The gymnosophists eye him with interest, eager to ask their own. “Tell us, King Alexander, what should a man do to be most loved?”
Seek power yet cause no fear.

Alexander's realm incorporates cities that once birthed civilizations. Their people hold customs that sometimes ignite polemics from more conservative citizens. While some worship kings as gods, others claim all men are equal.
Kings stand far above the common man.
Word has begun to spread that Alexander is blessed by the gods. Enemy lances slide past him, and the sky itself keeps arrows from his skin. The songs they sing of him please even the Muses.
Conquest is the only way to rule the world.
Poetry and song will make the world ask to be ours.
New lands and new kings inspire Alexander to press further. The bounds of his kingdom shall be the bounds of the world. Nothing less suits a hero.
The Muses watch from afar as the world changes hands.
War is all and good, but a little coin never hurt.
Recently, Alexander has heard his men speak of one of the enemy’s commanders. Hailing from the far continent, this general uses tactics never before seen. Facing him will be a worthy challenge.
A charge from the right flank will unsettle him.
Defeat an Army Commander in Distant Lands.

Alexander's new city glistens with promise, but some of the locals greet their new lord with skepticism. Urban amenities will showcase the benefits of his rule, but a grander display would remove any lingering doubt.
Construct something the world has never seen.
Build a Wonder in Distant Lands.
Coin can change minds just as easily.

An envoy from a neighboring town has come offering gifts to Alexander and to the {1_CIVILIZATION_ADJ} people. Though he has heard of the grandeur of Alexander’s cities, seeing it in truth stikes him speechless. Cities are for men, but this shines as for a god.
Mention the many positions open for talented souls.
Some kings rule by enforcing a uniformity of thought, but in Alexander’s mind, self-government is a reward for discipline. Noble minds have no need to be yoked by the collar of might.

Two friends are playing chess in the park, but the game captures the least of their attention. They bicker colorfully about an old riddle - what is the greatest extent of man? Finally, one stands and exclaims her answer. The other softly says, “Checkmate.”
A noble death ends a virtuous life.
Man is the most cunning of beasts.
Of all creatures, man is the most wicked.
+1 Resource Slot in Settlements with a Factory.
Each morning, Alexander wakes to yet still more of the world beyond his grasp. The horizon taunts him with lands not his own. His thirst for conquest will be sated.
Show the world its rightful ruler.
The edge of the world is so close now.

{1_CIVILIZATION_ADJ: gender *:an?An; other?A;} {1_CIVILIZATION_ADJ} composer has captured the nation’s imagination with their latest work - an opera that features the legend of Alexander, godlike hero, greatest of all men. Citizens and tourists alike are inspired to learn more of his exploits.
History and legend are now one.
As Alexander, access a new Exploration Dedication:[n] +15% Culture, -10% Science
As Alexander, access a new Exploration Dedication:[n] +3 Combat Strength for your Naval Units, -10% Influence
As Alexander, access a new Modern Dedication:[n] +15% Culture, -10% Science
As Alexander, access a new Modern Dedication:[n] +3 Combat Strength for your Air Units, -10% Influence
+15% Production towards constructing Buildings and Wonders with Great Works slots.
+5 Combat Strength for all Units against Districts.
[B]Alexander's[/B] empire covered much of the same territory as the [B]Abbasids[/B].
